text.title: Fine-Tuning: When to Fine-Tune vs Alternatives
text.summary: Decision tree for prompt engineering vs RAG vs fine-tuning vs RAFT; cost-benefit analysis; readiness criteria.

rule.statement: Follow this decision order: (1) Try prompt engineering first — if accuracy meets threshold with few-shot examples, ship it. (2) If knowledge is the bottleneck (private data, up-to-date facts), add RAG. (3) Only fine-tune if prompt + RAG still falls short AND you have 500+ quality examples AND the task pattern is stable.
rule.rationale: Prompt engineering costs nothing and ships in hours. RAG costs embed+query latency and vector DB infra but adds any knowledge base without training. Fine-tuning costs GPU time, data curation, and ongoing maintenance when the base model updates. Jumping to fine-tuning before exhausting cheaper options is the most common ML engineering mistake.

list.item: Prompt engineering: <1 day, no infra, limited by context window and base model capability
RAG: 1-5 days, vector DB required, best for knowledge-intensive tasks with changing data
Fine-tuning: 1-2 weeks+, GPU required, best for stable patterns, tone, format, specialized reasoning
RAFT (RAG + Fine-tuning): combines both; use when RAG recall is high but synthesis quality is poor

rule.statement: Do not start fine-tuning without verifying all four readiness criteria: (1) 500+ labeled examples with consistent quality, (2) clear eval metric that can be measured automatically, (3) task behavior is stable (will not change in 3 months), (4) prompt+RAG was tried and failed the eval metric.
rule.rationale: Each criterion prevents a specific failure mode. Without 500 examples: overfitting. Without an eval metric: no way to know if fine-tuning helped. With an unstable task: fine-tuned model goes stale and must be re-trained. Without trying prompt+RAG first: fine-tuning solves a problem that didn't need it.

list.item: Examples: minimum 500 for instruction following; 1,000+ for reliable specialization; 5,000+ for behavioral alignment
Eval metric: classification accuracy, BLEU/ROUGE, LLM-as-judge score — must be measurable before training
Task stability: task description unchanged for 6+ months AND no planned changes in 3 months
Prompt baseline: documented prompt+few-shot accuracy with score below acceptable threshold

rule.statement: Estimate total fine-tuning cost as: data_curation_cost + GPU_training_cost + eval_cost + maintenance_cost_per_year. Compare to ongoing RAG inference cost. Fine-tuning is cost-effective when inference volume is >100K calls/month at tasks where RAG reduces quality.
rule.rationale: Fine-tuned models have lower per-call cost (no retrieval overhead) but high upfront and maintenance cost. For high-volume, stable tasks with fixed knowledge, fine-tuning pays off. For low-volume or frequently updated knowledge, the upfront cost never amortizes.

example.code: def estimate_finetuning_roi( monthly_calls: int, rag_cost_per_call: float, # embed + LLM ft_cost_per_call: float, # LLM only (no retrieval) training_cost: float, # one-time GPU cost eval_cost: float, # monthly eval runs months: int = 12, ) -> dict: rag_total = monthly_calls * rag_cost_per_call * months ft_total = training_cost + (monthly_calls * ft_cost_per_call + eval_cost) * months return { "rag_total": rag_total, "finetuning_total": ft_total, "breakeven_months": training_cost / ((rag_cost_per_call - ft_cost_per_call) * monthly_calls), "preferred": "fine-tuning" if ft_total < rag_total else "RAG", }

list.item: Fewer than 100 labeled examples — use few-shot prompting instead
Task requirements change frequently — fine-tuned model will be stale within weeks
Knowledge-intensive task where data changes — RAG handles this better
No GPU access or budget for ongoing re-training — use prompt engineering + OpenAI fine-tuning API
Improving general reasoning or world knowledge — fine-tuning doesn't add new knowledge, it adjusts patterns
